policy_statement: Provide policy guidance and financial support to municipalities adopting building electrification and energy efficiency policies informed by city decarbonization plans (e.g., Ithaca).
mechanism_description: NYSERDA provides policy guidance and financial support to municipalities to adopt building electrification and energy efficiency policies, helping shift buildings away from fossil fuels and reduce energy use and associated emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Building energy use policy + finance support → electrification + efficiency upgrades → ↓ fossil fuel combustion + ↓ energy demand → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Municipalities adopt and implement building electrification and energy-efficiency policies/programs (often via codes, incentives, and retrofit initiatives) enabled by state-level guidance and funding.  
(2) Mediating process: Fuel switching reduces direct on-site combustion emissions; efficiency reduces delivered energy set for the same building services; emissions fall further as grid electricity decarbonizes.  
(3) Outcome: Lower operational (Scope 1 and often Scope 2) building-sector GHG emissions (and associated co-pollutants), contributing to mitigation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions from building energy use by replacing fossil-fuel combustion with efficient electric technologies and lowering total energy demand.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism reduces GHG emissions through building electrification and energy efficiency (fuel switching + demand reduction).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While efficiency can be framed as “Resource Efficiency,” the dominant climate effect across all instances here is emissions mitigation (the stated goal and main physical outcome is ↓ GHG emissions). Adaptation and nature-based pathways are not central to the mechanism.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a program were purely about saving energy costs without material emissions impact, Resource Efficiency could dominate; however, this canonical mechanism explicitly targets building decarbonization via electrification and efficiency, making mitigation primary.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy using this mechanism—guidance/financial support to implement electrification and efficiency—has its largest climate-relevant effect through reduced building operational emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., “electrification,” “retrofits,” “code,” “incentives”) is deferred to policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If electricity supply is highly carbon-intensive and efficiency/electrification is minimal or poorly adopted, near-term emissions reductions may be smaller; nevertheless, the intended and typical dominant pathway remains mitigation via reduced fossil combustion and lower energy demand.
key_indicators: “provide policy guidance and financial support”; “municipalities that adopt building electrification and energy efficiency policies”; “decarbonization plans”; “ambitious commitments”
co_benefits: Improved indoor/outdoor air quality; lower energy bills/energy burden; local jobs/workforce development; improved building comfort and public health.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a planning-strategy/technical assistance measure, but the explicit “financial support” component makes “incentive-program” the best single instrument type; the action is enabling because it supports municipalities rather than directly requiring electrification/efficiency outcomes.
